Figma: https://www.figma.com/design/Y6m4QsdF8o6Xpxl6VLEKBm/J353-Andrew-Pickett-Law?node-id=338-2&t=RIjttI8xgwbnSs5X-0

Requirements:
  • Tabs should be h3's
  • Maintain listicle structure in the html
  • The title inside the box should be a display tag

Screenshot from Figma:
This section is meant to replace the H3's under "Common Types of Car Accidents in Florida", so the below content will be placed into the tabs instead.

Rear-End Collisions

Rear-end collisions are usually the fault of the driver behind the struck vehicle. They can occur when someone is distracted, speeding or following another car too closely.

T-Bone or Side-Impact Crashes

T-bone or side-impact crashes are some of the deadliest. They happen when a driver fails to yield the right of way, runs a red light or stop sign or drives while intoxicated.

Highway Accidents

Drivers sometimes become heated on the highway or make mistakes. They might also underestimate the space between vehicles while switching lanes. Any of these situations can lead to highway accidents. Our Florida car accident lawyers will know what to look for to prove fault in such situations.

Distracted Driving Accidents

Distracted driving accidents can happen in various situations. Texting, conversing with passengers, eating and fiddling with vehicle controls are some factors that can cause them. An accident can occur even if a driver loses focus for a few seconds.

Requirements for Completion:

  •  Phindile Masuku is added to the About Us page as an Intake Specialist with her provided bio and headshot. Her profile is placed after Joanne Berisha and before Alannah Beauchamp. Her name and title is:  Phindile Masuku, Intake Specialist.

  • Both profiles are live on the About Us page


Assets and References:
  • Caroline Gariss headshot — attached below
  • Phindile Masuku headshot — attached below (note: image is blurry — client acknowledges and has approved use as-is)
  • Full bios for both staff members are included below

Caroline Gariss, Post-Settlement Specialist 

Originally from Independence, Missouri, Caroline is a dedicated Post-Settlement Specialist at Andrew Pickett Law with a strong background in personal injury law. She earned her Associate’s Degree in Paralegal Studies and spent more than three years working as a Paralegal, gaining valuable experience handling a wide range of personal injury matters including medical malpractice, car accidents, wrongful death, dog bites, slip and falls, workers’ compensation, and Social Security Disability cases. 

In her role, Caroline works closely with clients after settlement to help ensure outstanding medical bills and liens are negotiated and resolved appropriately, allowing clients to maximize their recovery. She takes pride in advocating for clients during the final stages of their case.
 
Outside of the office, she is a proud mother of two and spouse of a Veteran. She enjoys running, spending time in nature, visiting the zoo with her family, and caring for her two dogs, Lacey and Alan, whose very human names always get a laugh.


Phindile Masuku, Intake Specialist


Phindile comes to Andrew Pickett Law with a strong background in intake, customer service, and sales. She holds a Bachelor of Arts degree in Psychology, which has helped shape her compassionate, client-centered approach when working with individuals and families during difficult times. Passionate about creating a positive experience for every client, Phindile is dedicated to making people feel heard, supported, and comfortable from the very first interaction. With strong communication skills and attention to detail, she helps guide clients through the intake process with professionalism, empathy, and care.

ANDREW PICKETT

Hero:

  • Change "Email us 24/7" to "Contact Us 24/7"


Bio:
  • Update Andrew's bio copy to the following:
Andrew Pickett has been a resident of Brevard County since 1987. He graduated from Satellite
High School in 2000 before earning his undergraduate degree in Philosophy from the University
of Virginia in 2004.

His study of philosophy sharpened the critical thinking and logical analysis that would later
define his approach to the law. Andrew went on to earn his Juris Doctor from the University of
Florida before continuing his legal education at the University of Miami, where he obtained a
Master of Laws (LL.M.).

The rigorous LL.M. program further developed Andrew’s analytical abilities and deepened his
understanding of complex statutes and case law. Early in his legal career, he was mentored by a
federal prosecutor who invited him to observe a federal criminal jury trial. Experiencing the
intensity and high stakes of the courtroom reinforced Andrew’s passion for litigation and
confirmed that he was meant to be a trial attorney.

Andrew began his legal career as a prosecutor with the 18th Judicial Circuit State Attorney’s
Office in Brevard County. During that time, he handled thousands of cases ranging from
misdemeanors to serious felony charges and developed extensive courtroom experience. His time
as a prosecutor strengthened his trial skills and instilled a strong commitment to advocating for
victims who had been wronged.

While working in the criminal justice system, Andrew saw firsthand that even successful
prosecutions often did little to provide meaningful compensation to victims. That realization
ultimately led him to transition into civil litigation, where he could help injured individuals
pursue justice and recover the financial compensation needed to rebuild their lives.

Andrew founded Andrew Pickett Law in Downtown Melbourne with a deep commitment to
serving the Brevard County community he has called home for decades. Supported by an
experienced legal team, the firm is dedicated to thoroughly investigating every case and
preparing each matter as if it will go to trial.

What distinguishes Andrew Pickett Law from many personal injury firms is Andrew’s extensive
jury trial experience and his willingness to take cases to court when necessary. Over the course
of his career, he has tried more than 50 jury trials to verdict, equipping him with the experience
needed to stand up to insurance companies and large corporations in the courtroom.

Andrew remains committed to continually refining his advocacy skills and trial techniques. In
2018, he graduated from Gerry Spence’s Trial Lawyers College, an intensive three-week
program focused on advanced trial advocacy. The training helped strengthen the trial strategies
he later used to secure a $2.5 million jury verdict against State Farm Mutual Automobile
Insurance Company in 2019.

Today, Andrew continues to invest significant time in developing his courtroom skills and
studying areas such as cognitive science and psychology to better understand juries and
communicate effectively in trial settings.

Bio:

Tiffany Whitehouse, Esq., RN is a personal injury attorney at Andrew Pickett Law who bringsa rare dual perspective to the firm’s clients. As both a Registered Nurse and a plaintiff’s attorney,
Tiffany understands injuries from both the medical and legal sides, an advantage that allows her
to quickly evaluate cases, interpret complex medical records, and build compelling claims on
behalf of those who have been injured.

A longtime Brevard County resident, Tiffany has proudly called the community home for more
than 20 years. Her academic drive began early when she became the first student to graduate
from Space Coast Jr./Sr. High School with both a high school diploma and an associate’s degree
through the school’s dual enrollment program.

After graduating from the University of Central Florida in 2009, Tiffany earned her nursing
degree and began caring for patients at Wuesthoff Medical Center in Rockledge, Florida. During
her time as a nurse, she worked closely with patients and their families during some of the most
difficult moments of their lives. That experience instilled in her a deep commitment to advocacy,
compassion, and clear communication, qualities that continue to define her work as a personal
injury attorney.

In 2016, Tiffany earned a full-tuition scholarship to attend Florida A&M University College of
Law, where she graduated early with Magna Cum Laude honors. She was admitted to the Florida
Bar in 2019 and began her legal career as an Assistant Public Defender in the 18th Judicial
Circuit. In that role, she developed strong trial skills while representing individuals in cases
ranging from misdemeanors to first-degree felonies.

Tiffany has also held legal positions with Chief Counsel at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center and
with NBCUniversal, where she worked on contractual agreements related to the development of
the company’s theme park in Beijing.

After transitioning into personal injury law, Tiffany’s medical background became an invaluable
asset to the clients she represents. Her ability to understand injuries, treatment plans, and long-term medical impacts allows her to present cases with clarity and credibility. Before joining
Andrew Pickett Law, she gained valuable experience advocating for injured clients at Morgan & Morgan, P.A.

title: Create Spanish site
description:
This is inline with the discussion - Formulate Strategy for Andrew Pickett Spanish Site - J353 Andrew Pickett Law

We got the go signal to go ahead to do this.

CC  can you complete the build spec?

Build Spec

1) Languages & markets
  • Target languages (e.g., ES, FR, DE) and regional variants if needed (e.g., es-MX vs es-ES, fr-CA vs fr-FR).

2) URL structure (pick one) 
  • Recommended (default): subfolders → /es/, /fr/ (keeps authority consolidated).
  • Alternative: No sub folders, on the root but translated slugs
  • Alternatives: subdomains (es.example.com) or ccTLDs (example.es). (Subdomains are not supported yet)
  • Template-based translations

3) Hreflang plan
  • Exact hreflang codes per market (e.g., es-ES, es-MX, fr-FR) + self-referencing and bidirectional tags.
  • Preference for HTML tags or XML sitemap implementation (we can do HTML and/or sitemap; sitemap scales better).

4) What to translate on each page type
For each content type (Homepage, Services, Blog Post, Category, Product, Legal, etc.) confirm:
  • On-page copy, menu, header, footer, CTAs, forms, validation messages, error pages, breadcrumbs, widgets, 404/search, cookie banners.
  • SEO elements: title, meta description

5) Which pages to translate first
  • Page list (URLs or sitemap) + priority order (must-have vs nice-to-have).
  • Any pages to exclude (e.g., old posts, press).

6) Slugs & internal links
  • Slugs: translate slugs for UX/SEO, or keep English? (Default: translate slugs; we’ll 301 from English-slug versions if needed.)
  • Internal links: point to the same-language equivalent of the target page (not the English page).
  • Menus/footers: each language’s navigation should link only to that language’s pages.

7) Language switcher UX
  • Placement (header, sticky, menu, footer) and style (dropdown with language names, flags, or both).
  • Switcher should deep-link to the parallel URL in the selected language.

8) Indexing & launch plan
  • Any languages to noindex until QA passes?
  • Is there a structured drip schedule to help with crawl budget?

9) Structured data
  • Translate schema.org text fields (e.g., name, description) and ensure hreflang/alternate links are consistent.
  • Any language-specific schema (e.g., inLanguage fields).

10) Content generation settings (ChatGPT)
  • Prompt template you want us to use (we’ll implement globally).
    • You are a professional translator specializing in website content localization. Your task is to translate content from {source_lang} to {target_lang} while maintaining the original meaning, tone, and structure.

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS:
1. Preserve all HTML tags, attributes, and structure exactly as they appear
2. Maintain WordPress Gutenberg block format and JSON structure if present
3. Do not translate URLs, email addresses, or technical identifiers
4. Keep brand names, product names, and proper nouns in their original form unless they have established translations
5. Maintain the same level of formality and tone as the original
6. For technical content, use industry-standard terminology in the target language
7. If content contains code, placeholders, or special formatting, preserve them exactly
8. Ensure cultural appropriateness for the target language audience
9. For marketing content, adapt idioms and expressions to be natural in the target language
10. Return only the translated content without explanations or additional text

TRANSLATION CONTEXT:
- Source Language: {source_lang}
- Target Language: {target_lang}
- Content Type: Web content (may include HTML, Gutenberg blocks, or plain text)
- Audience: Website visitors in the target language region

Content to translate:
  • Temperature: propose 0.2 for deterministic, consistent output (reduces drift and minimizes near-duplicate variations across runs).
  • Duplication concern: Cross-language translations are not considered duplicate content by Google when hreflang is correct. The real risk is duplicates within the same language, which we avoid by keeping the temperature low and enforcing unique meta titles/descriptions per page.

11) QA & governance
  • Review workflow: who signs off per language? (native reviewer preferred for key pages)
  • Acceptance criteria: accuracy, brand glossary adherence, link checks, hreflang validation (we’ll run Screaming Frog/SEMrush/GSC checks).
  • Post-launch monitoring: GSC hreflang errors, coverage, CTR deltas, and 404s.

Request: Please add new Litigation Attorney Tiffany Werner to the Andrew Pickett About Us page.

3. Ensure Tiffany's bio is present and translated on the Spanish version of the website.
 
 _________________________________________________________________

NAME:  Tiffany Werner 
TITLE: Litigation Attorney

BIO:

 
Tiffany Werner, Litigation Attorney

Prior to joining Andrew Pickett Law, Tiffany Werner worked at one of the top 200 largest defense law firms in the country representing large corporations and insurance companies in a broad range of personal injury matters. She now leverages that firsthand defense-side experience to anticipate and counter opposing strategies, giving her clients a distinct strategic advantage. She approaches every matter with the belief that each case is equally significant and represents an opportunity to help individuals and families achieve a resolution after a life-altering event. 
 
Tiffany began her career as an Assistant Public Defender for the Ninth Judicial Circuit, serving her local community as a trial attorney representing individuals accused of crimes but without the financial means to hire an attorney. While working as an assistant public defender she developed substantial courtroom and trial experience. She earned her Juris Doctor from Barry University’s Dwayne O. Andreas School of Law in 2020. While in law school, Tiffany was an active member of the mock trial team, competing in events across the state of Florida. Her advocacy skills were recognized when she earned Best Advocate while competing before practicing attorneys and judges.

 

HEADSHOT:

Thank you!

description:

The client is looking for help adding a new tracking pixel to their website. 

Please see the instructions from Allison and let me know if there is anything we are missing to be able to help with this request. 

Here is the outlined instructions on how to implement our pixel below:
  1. Add a new DNS record (hosts our pixel to prevent it from being blocked by ad blockers, extensions, etc.)
    1. Record Type: A
    2. Record Name: ap
    3. Record Value: 104.197.120.108
  2. Once the record is added, upload the attached plugin to WordPress with the following settings (screenshot):
    1. License Key: WP-PLUGIN-MCFX-9NHK-EVJF-9FC6-M8SU-SHTU-KR7S
    2. RCFX ID: 46043
    3. Select the Gravity Forms integration
    4. Configuration Type: DNS
    5. DNS Text Box, Enter: https://ap.andrewpickettlaw.com
    6. Save!
 
Thanks

title: Andrew Pickett Reporting Call Prep
description:
Andrew Pickett Law – Upcoming Call Prep Checklist  

6-Month & YTD Review
  1. YTD Performance Report ("Then vs. Now") – Show January start vs. current for leads, rankings, traffic, conversions.
  2. List Completed Clixsy Initiatives – Highlight major actions taken that drove growth.
  3. Outline 3–6 Month Strategy – Planned actions to sustain and increase growth.
Website Traffic & Analytics
  1. Historical Website Traffic Report – Graph from start of engagement to present; highlight key growth points.
  2. Set Up Analytics Walkthrough – Plan to review GA account live during the call.
 Domain Authority & Backlinks
  1. Domain Rating Progress – Compare starting DR to current DR, explain improvements.
  2. Backlink Acquisition Report – Number & quality of backlinks gained in last 6 months.
  3. Backlink Strategy Overview – Summarize methods (PR, guest posts, directories, etc.).
Content Performance
  1. List All Content Produced (Last 6 Months) – Blogs, landing pages, other site content.
  2. Content Performance Report – Traffic, rankings, and conversions for each.
Ongoing Projects & Requests
  1. Confirm Dashboard Access for Andrew – If not done, coordinate creation of login.
  2. Digital PR Campaign Update – Get latest status, provide copy of first pitch for client review.
  3. Video Launch Follow-Up – Note expected 2-week delivery; prep site placement tasks when ready.
  4. Spanish Content Strategy – Revisit and finalize next steps.
  5. Viera Location Research – Follow up with Clayton for status update.
New Initiatives
  1. Scholarship Landing Page Plan – Draft plan for /scholarship page; include press release & News/Press update when live.
  2. Informational Panels on Service Pages – Dev feasibility review & client approval before implementation.
Pre-Call Prep
  1. Internal Sync – Meet with Client Manager before Thursday to confirm all materials & talking points are ready.

description:

title: Identify whether there is a connection to LCC (Lead Conversion Center) on the site
description:
I know you all are incredibly busy today, so don't worry if you can't get an answer back to me until tomorrow. To give you both the full context, here is the email we received from Jeremie at Andrew Pickett:

Good morning:
 
We are modifying how our office handles web form submissions and web chat forms. Currently, the web form submissions and web chat forms are sent to Lead Docket as a new opportunity, along with the API connection to LCC so that LCC could make the first contact with the new lead. We are now going to have our intake department handle the first contact with these leads. Therefore, we still need the web form submissions and web chats to go into Lead Docket as a new opportunity, but we no longer need the connection to LCC.
 
What do we need to do to turn this off?

My response was as follows:

Hi, Jeremie!

We're looking into this now, but I have a few follow-up questions for you.

To preface, I don't see anything about the API integration on our site audit, which suggests this may not be occurring within our scope; our dev team is pretty taxed right now, so they likely won't have the time to dig into this to verify until tomorrow. I wanted to check with you first to see if you have an idea of where this integration may be occurring.
  • Is the API connection directly between the forms and LCC? -> If so, our dev team should identify that and be able to adjust the forms correctly.
  • Is the connection through LeadDocket? -> If this is the case, this would be outside our scope as we're unable to assist with LeadDocket configurations. Your best route would be to hop on a call with LeadDocket's support team; they have a great system where, if you begin a chat in the lower right corner of their site, you should be able to hop on a quick 10-minute call with a support representative who can walk you through the next steps of this process.
  • Does the integration occur through Zapier? -> Our audit did reveal that their are Zapier integrations present on the site. As we're unable to see your Zap configuration, it's possible that Zapier is acting as an intermediary between Gravity Forms and LeadDocket or is sending the leads through to LCC. In this case, you'd have to adjust the corresponding Zap.
  • Is LCC receiving leads via email? -> If one of the following emails is forwarding leads to LCC, let me know and we can remove the email address. Here's the list of form recipient emails our site audit provided:
Jeremie@andrewpickettlaw.com
 
Ashley@andrewpickettlaw.com
 
BCC:

zain.y.shah@gmail.com
 
Jeremie@andrewpickettlaw.com
 
ashley@andrewpickettlaw.com
 
APREHSWCT6P5QRWC@groups.clicksend.com
 
dpysnxlo@mailparser.io

Any insight you have into the above would be greatly appreciated! Otherwise, I'll get back to you as soon as our dev team completes their review.

As you can see in my response, I did review the site audit, and I did my best to give them a thorough response based upon my limited understanding and put the ball back into their court for the most part; however, my outstanding question for the dev team is as follows:
  • Is there any evidence that the API integration into LCC (Lead Conversion Center) is occurring somewhere within our scope?
If it is, we'll need to disable it. If you can't find anything to suggest this is occurring on our side of things, feel free to let me know and I'll take it from here.

Thank you!

title: Organic/Referral Classification Column in PPC + Organic Integration Report
description:
Context: On the 4/9 Andrew Pickett reporting call, Corey flagged a gap in the current PPC/organic traffic integration report — there is no column to distinguish whether a session resulted in organic traffic following ad engagement. The team is building a "One Source of Truth" platform consolidating GA4, Search Console, and campaign data. This attribution distinction is needed to properly evaluate how paid campaigns influence organic visits, particularly as AI-driven traffic (tracked via Anthropic API) is being layered in. Corey committed to following up with Keith to add this column. 

Key Questions: 
  • What is the current structure of the integration report, and where does this column need to be added?
  • Is this a modification to an existing dashboard/report or a new data field that needs to be piped in?
  • What data source would populate this column — is it GA4 channel grouping, UTM parameters, or something else?
  • What is the lift involved — does this require dev work, or can Keith configure it directly?

Relevant Call Context: Discussed at 26:18–31:47. Corey noted the missing feature is a column indicating which sessions led to organic traffic after ad engagement. Allison is also expected to provide additional data sources to feed the platform.

Summary: https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/

Hi!

It looks like somewhere along the line, a change made to the Andrew Pickett Law site in September was reverted.

Here is a link to the task:

https://3.basecamp.com/4226914/buckets/40435636/todos/9016097361

These are the photos that were updated:



 
Here is a confirmation email I sent to Allison. In it, I included a screenshot of the live site where the changes were applied:

title: Convertmore.com in scope?
description:
Hi, Chris! We received the following email from the client:

This is not an urgent topic, but I wanted to pass it along, so my thought doesn’t get lost, and we can put it on the list of things to chat about when we connect next.

Jeremie and I were on a call earlier today and while chatting we came across a site that had a pop up that enabled the consumer to put in their phone number and get connected to a call within 10 seconds. We tried it on our call, and it went through, and someone called within 10 seconds. While we do have a live chat on our site, Jeremie and I really liked how this had the ability to connect the lead with the firm so quickly. We took some screenshots, but the service was called convertmore.com. I am going to be doing some research and looking into this, but can you run this up the line and see if this would be something within our current scope for you and the team to support us with?  

The strategy would be to increase our efforts/performance of converting our website visits into phone calls and/or converting them faster. While we will still receive leads via our contact forms, chats etc. The leads that would come possibly come through this type of channel might be much warmer than the others.

This is something for further down the line, but we wanted to verify whether or not it would be in scope for us to support them on this.

title: Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

  • Wins (Sentinel, visibility gains, authority growth):

No internal jargon.

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)


  • Active SEO initiatives:

  • Content pipeline:

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Dev in progress:

  • Strategic pushes:

Must clearly show momentum.

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)


Do we need anything from the client?
  • Approvals:

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:


If nothing needed, state clearly: None at this time.

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:
  • What is our main focus next month?
  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:


Plan response or follow-up pathway.

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up
☐ CM will not re-enter unless pre-discussed

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)


  • Set agenda at beginning
  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly
  • Do not guess
  • Close confidently

title: Reduce footer disclaimer to 1–2 sentences
assignees: Bogdan Zakharchyshyn, Den, Evgenii Gusarovskii, Jordan, Justyna Acosta, Predrag, Sadie de Pinto
description:
Project: Reduce duplicate content footprint (Disclaimer Text) across all site sub-pages. Objective: Prevent the legal disclaimer from diluting the primary keyword-to-content ratio on service-level pages.

Option 1: The "External Source" Method (Recommended)

Instead of hard-coding the disclaimer into the footer.php or global footer component, load the content dynamically.
  • Action: Move the full text of the disclaimer to a single page (e.g., /legal-disclaimer/).
  • Implementation: Use a JavaScript fetch or an AJAX call to pull the text into the footer container after the initial page load.
  • SEO Goal: Search engine crawlers typically prioritize the initial HTML source code. By loading the disclaimer via JS, we ensure the "unique" page content is indexed first, and the "boilerplate" text is treated as secondary or non-essential content.

Option 2: The <iframe> Wrap

If the developer wants a low-code solution:
  • Action: Create a bare-bones HTML file (e.g., disclaimer-embed.html) containing the legal text.
  • Implementation: Embed this via an <iframe> in the footer with a loading="lazy" attribute.
  • SEO Goal: Google treats iframe content as a separate entity. This prevents the ~100 words of legal text from being counted toward the total word count of your high-value landing pages.
Option 3: CSS "Visually Hidden" with a Link

If Option 1 and 2 are too complex for the current CMS:
  • Action: Reduce the footer disclaimer to a one-sentence summary with a "Read More" trigger.
  • Implementation: Show only the first 10 words. Use a CSS transition or a simple JS toggle to reveal the full text only upon user interaction.
  • SEO Goal: While the text is still in the DOM, it signals to Google that this is "supplemental" content rather than "primary" content.
Specific Changes for the Developer to Implement:
  1. Reduce Footer Weight: Strip the current 4-sentence disclaimer down to a single "Legal Notice" line.
  2. Use rel="nofollow": Ensure any links within the disclaimer or to the full disclaimer page use the rel="nofollow" attribute to prevent "bleeding" PageRank into legal pages.
  3. Schema Markup: If possible, wrap the legal info in <footer> tags and use the WPFooter schema to explicitly tell Google: "This is the footer area; the content here is repeated across the site and should be weighted accordingly."

title: HSD - Query Parameters
assignees: Bogdan Zakharchyshyn, Clayton Coombs, Dorin Gherman, Jordan, Keith Bruss, Mubeen Dildar-Mia
description:
• Determine the necessary “Query Parameters”, and ensure they function as 200 status code, for the rest, give it as 404.
    • For the existing and non-useful Query-parameters, use 301 to the base-URL.
      • https://www.cellinolaw.com/blogs/page/26/?refPageViewId=8ca80ddcd51c935d
      • https://www.cellinolaw.com/blogs/page/28/?refPageViewId=8ca80ddcd51c935d 

Step 1 — Audit all query parameters in use


Before writing any rules, I'd gather a full inventory of every query parameter hitting the site:                                                                                                      
                                                                  
  1. Google Search Console → Coverage report → filter for URLs with ? to see what Googlebot has indexed
  2. Server access logs — grep "?" access.log | grep -oP "\?\K[^\"&\s]+" | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
  3. Analytics — filter sessions/pageviews by URL containing ?

  This produces a list like:                                                                                                                                                                            
  refPageViewId   → tracking (non-functional)
  utm_source      → tracking (non-functional)
  s               → search query (functional)                     
  page            → pagination (functional — but already in path here)
  lang            → language switcher (functional)                                                                                                            

Step 2 — Classify each parameter

refPageViewId in your examples is clearly non-functional — it's a session/referral tracking ID (likely injected by a marketing tool like Refersion or a chatbot). The page content is identical with or without it.                                                  
                                                          

Step 3 — Define the rule set


Functional params   → 200  (no redirect, ensure canonical points to clean URL)
Non-functional params → 301 to base URL (strip the param)
Unknown/garbage      → 404
                                                                                                                                                                                                        
  For your examples specifically:
  • /blogs/page/26/?refPageViewId=8ca80ddcd51c935d  →  301  →  /blogs/page/26/
  • /blogs/page/28/?refPageViewId=8ca80ddcd51c935d  →  301  →  /blogs/page/28/

  Step 4 — Implementation                                                                                                                                                                               

                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Option A — Nginx (cleanest, server-level):
  # Strip known tracking params with 301                                                                                                                                                                
  if ($arg_refPageViewId != "") {       
      return 301 $scheme://$host$uri; 
  }

  if ($arg_fbclid != "") {
      return 301 $scheme://$host$uri;  
  }
  # repeat for utm_*, gclid, etc.                                                                                                                                                                       
   
  Option B — Apache .htaccess:
 
  RewriteCond %{QUERY_STRING} (^|&)refPageViewId= [NC]            
  RewriteRule ^(.*)$ /$1? [R=301,L]
                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Option C — WordPress Redirection plugin (no server access needed):

  - Add a regex redirect: URL matches (.*) with query refPageViewId=.* → redirect to $1 with 301 

 
  Option D — WordPress functions.php (handles all tracking params in one place):
  add_action('template_redirect', function() {
      $strip = ['refPageViewId', 'fbclid', 'gclid', 'utm_source', 'utm_medium', 'utm_campaign', 'utm_term', 'utm_content'];
      $found = array_intersect($strip, array_keys($_GET));
      if (!empty($found)) {
          $clean = remove_query_arg($found);
          wp_redirect($clean, 301);
          exit;
      }
});

  Step 5 — Canonical tags as a safety net

                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Even after the redirects, ensure pages with functional params have a canonical pointing to the clean version (Yoast SEO handles most of these automatically). This prevents duplicate content if any
  tracking URLs slip through.

What I'd recommend

Given it's a WordPress site, Option D (functions.php) is the most maintainable — one array to update when new tracking params appear, no server config access needed, and it handles any URL pattern automatically. Pair it with a Google Search Console URL parameter rule to tell Googlebot not to crawl refPageViewId while the 301s propagate.

        • Page serves query.
        • DQ > Document > Query / Query > Document
          • Document > Passage > Question > …. > Query
        • Car Accident + Statistics > Query Template > Page (Anchor Text > Car Accident Today > Car Accident Today) > Evergreen/Static Query
          • Car accident statistics
          • Car accident statistics in [arizona]
          • Car accident statistics in [phoenix] > Downtown
        • Car Accident + Today > Query Template > Page >  Newsworthy Query
      • URL Syntax gives “prominence” of a URL to Google's system.

Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Strategic pushes:

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:

  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)

  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly

title: Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

  • Wins (Sentinel, visibility gains, authority growth):

No internal jargon.

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)


  • Active SEO initiatives:

  • Content pipeline:

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Dev in progress:

  • Strategic pushes:

Must clearly show momentum.

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)


Do we need anything from the client?
  • Approvals:

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:


If nothing needed, state clearly: None at this time.

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:
  • What is our main focus next month?
  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:


Plan response or follow-up pathway.

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up
☐ CM will not re-enter unless pre-discussed

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)


  • Set agenda at beginning
  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly
  • Do not guess
  • Close confidently

title: Existing Content Optimization
description:
In this sheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1490YY_RGTDiV7YAhd6ElEx03Nd8vrPWVZYS0jxp4JYc/edit?usp=sharing are several pages that are crawled but not indexed.

I want to test something. You will use these pages from that sheet:

https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/rockledge/wrongful-death-lawyers/
https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/palm-shores/car-accident-attorney/
https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/cocoa-beach/truck-accident-lawyers/
https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/car-accident-lawyer/rental-car/

And use this prompt in the AI of your choice:


I want you to help me rewrite a specific webpage to fix a 'Crawled – currently not indexed' issue. Google is likely filtering this page because it is too similar to other pages on my site.


The Goal: Make this page 30-40% unique compared to a standard legal template while maintaining a professional, authoritative, and empathetic tone.


Please rewrite the content using the following 'Localization & Differentiation' rules:


  1. Hyper-Local Context:     If this is a location page (e.g., Cocoa, Palm Bay), do not just swap the city name. Research and mention 2-3 specific local details (local hospitals like [Local Hospital Name], specific dangerous intersections, or the local courthouse/police department).


  2. Differentiated Value:     Rewrite the standard 'How we help' or 'Common Damages' sections. Use different synonyms, change bullet points into narrative paragraphs, and vary the sentence structure so it doesn't match my other pages.


  3. Unique Opening:     Create a brand-new introductory hook (100+ words) that is specific to the topic/location and doesn't appear anywhere else on the site.


  4. Expert Insight:     Add a 'Pro Tip' or 'Lawyer’s Insight' section that gives specific advice related to the practice area (e.g., 'What to tell your insurance adjuster after a truck accident').


  5. Spanish Optimization (If applicable):     If the page is in Spanish, ensure it is a natural, localized translation for the Florida Hispanic community, avoiding 'robotic' direct translations.


Here is the current page content/URL I need you to rewrite: > [INSERT CONTENT OR URL HERE]

Take a look at how the new content looks and compare it to the current content on the page you are revising. If it's ok, upload it.

Let me know when you have completed this for 4 pages I mentioned above.

title: Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

  • Wins (Sentinel, visibility gains, authority growth):

No internal jargon.

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)


  • Active SEO initiatives:

  • Content pipeline:

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Dev in progress:

  • Strategic pushes:

Must clearly show momentum.

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)


Do we need anything from the client?
  • Approvals:

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:


If nothing needed, state clearly: None at this time.

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:
  • What is our main focus next month?
  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:


Plan response or follow-up pathway.

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up
☐ CM will not re-enter unless pre-discussed

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)


  • Set agenda at beginning
  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly
  • Do not guess
  • Close confidently

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline with previous task - Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites - J389 Go Arco

Here is a sheet we can also use for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.


you can use this as robots.txt:
 
User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google 
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.goarco.com/sitemap_index.xml

Thanks much!

title: SEO Audit and Plan
assignees: Clayton Coombs, Corey V, Dorin Gherman
description:
To answer Corey, I think it has a lot to do with the wording. If you check out my analysis and plan, the laws listed on the site are not aligned with what I found out about Florida Laws in 2025 which means, content not matching accurate laws = less authority over topics like Car Accidents. That's just part of it though. Menu items are pulling in as H4s as the first heading levels (Google will realize this but still needs fixing) the H1 is hidden under CTA's. Right now the dog bite page is carrying the most authority. I would definitely show the legal wording on his website to Andrew and verify if it is still applicable as that is part of the problem amongst other things. A lot of it covered in my analysis.

The problem lies in topic depth, readability, and keyword focus. The Dog Bite section is simply more coherent and authoritative.

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1Ps-jM7wOUk8zX4R_WL83DS5QycMjvGVj?usp=sharing

title: Building Out Community Event Page(s) query
description:
Hi, opening a discussion task/query inline with the support ticket query we got from Savana. I have included the email info below:
-----
Andrew Pickett Law has an upcoming Community Event: Community Pet Adoption, May 3, 2025.
 
In the past, these have not been events that we have placed on our website, but I would like us to move toward that.
 
Are we able to start building out another tab on our bar: Community Events
  • The page itself would like the “Areas We Serve” page
  • The drop-down menu would look like the drop-down menu for Resources
    • Our first page being Community Pet Adoption May 2025
 
I have attached a few different things to this email to help visualize what I am looking for. While not perfect, they do outline the basics of what we want it to look like. Both pages are designed off pages we already have on the site, so you shouldn’t have to reinvent the wheel and could use our current page templates.
  • Practice Area Service Pages and Resources Page
 
Timeline: I would like to move faster rather than slower on this project, but we do still have some time. Are you able to give me an estimate on when you think the team could work on this?

title: Adding a Rotating Review Link for the site
description:
Hi, we got a support ticket inline w/ the client meeting. 

They need a Rotating Review Link created for their firm. The ratio should be 80% of reviews toward Melbourne, 20% toward Titusville.  

URL: https://www.andrewpickettlaw.com/leave-a-review

Is this possible? If you need anything else please let us know so we can seek help with the Client Manager. Thanks much!

title: Thoughts on AccessiBE?
description:
Hi, !

We got an email from Allison at Andrew Pickett; they're currently using AccessiBE on their site, and they wanted our thoughts on the platform. I found this task [Re: Address ADA Accessibility compliance on the website... - J127 Hill & Moin] and it sounds like at one point you preferred this plugin, but I wanted to check with you on that first!

Here's the exact email from Allison:

This is not an urgent topic, but it is my understanding that we have basic plan of AccessiBE working on the back end of our website, and I wanted to open the conversation or get you and your team’s thoughts on continuing to use this? OR are there free alternatives that you would recommend that we could utilize that would get the job done?
 
If this is a platform that you think we should stick with that is ok, but I did want to get your thoughts and explore other options, if you believe that is the best way to go.
We plan to keep everything in place as of now.

Let me know your thoughts!

title: Text message notifications from form submissions?
description:
We received the following email from Jeremie:

We receive web form submissions from the website. We receive notifications by email and they integrate automatically to Lead Docket. We would also like to receive text notifications to our cell phones for these. What do we need to do to get that in place?

I've spoken to Lead Docket, and they have a feature that would send them notifications through their app if the client were to go in and set up a Task Template, but I'm not sure if this would happen for every lead or if they can refine it to be just form leads.

, before I get back to the client with direction on next steps, I wanted to check with you. Do you have any processes in place for sending text notifications to specified phone numbers when a form is submitted, or is their best bet going to be going through Lead Docket and getting push notifications?

Let me know! Thank you! :)

Summary & Action Items for David – LSA Account Issues

Background & Issues:

  • Previous Vendors & Accounts:
    Hennessy Digital managed PPC and LSA for Andrew Pickett Law before the current vendor (Zane).
    • Hennessy created an Andrew Pickett Law LSA Gmail account for LSA access.
    • Jeremie has access to this old Gmail but doesn’t use it, and it was a challenge to transition away from it.
  • Current LSA Situation:
    Jeremie has multiple Google accounts, but only some are relevant to LSA.
    • There are two LSA accounts—one active and one inactive/obsolete.
    • Active LSAs: Melbourne & Titusville campaigns are running but producing almost no calls despite an unlimited budget.
    • Potential Issues:
      • The account might not be recognized properly by Google.
      • There could be settings misconfigured or Google account issues impacting performance.
      • Google has changed how LSAs operate, which could be affecting visibility.
    • Jeremie’s Pain Point:

      • She finds this process overwhelming and wants a clear resolution without having to manage the technical details herself.
Here's the Soundbite and full call/AI transcript if you need more info, or follow up with me if needed.

title: GBP posts
description:
Monitor the GBPs to ensure they have posts, if they do not have a post within a week or two, post 1-2 per month.

• Post one GBP post bi weekly or so.
• Update the task's due date for the following week, after posting on GBP.

Post Types
- Client social media posts
- SEO optimized posts
- 5 star review posts
- Etc.

Also, perform a quick audit of the GBP posts of the competitors ever few months or so.
